screens.body: An energy detector just asks "is there more power here than the expected noise floor?" — at low enough SNR, the burst's power is genuinely indistinguishable from a noise fluctuation, and the energy detector misses it. A cyclostationary detector asks a fundamentally different question: "is there significant correlation between spectral components separated by a candidate cycle frequency α?" (spectral redundancy, from the core-theory module). Thermal noise and most interference are well-modeled as stationary — they have essentially **no** spectral redundancy at any α ≠ 0. So even when the burst's *power* is buried under the noise floor, its *cyclic correlation* at the right α can still stand out clearly, because noise contributes almost nothing there for the detector to compete against. This is why cyclostationary detectors are the standard tool for non-cooperative spectrum sensing at SNRs where energy detection has already given up.
screens.selfExplainPrompt: In your own words: why can a cyclostationary detector succeed at an SNR where an energy detector fails, even though both are looking at the same noisy recording?
screens.selfExplainAnswer: A strong answer notes that the two detectors are testing different things: the energy detector compares total power to a noise floor, and at low SNR the signal's power addition is too small to distinguish from noise fluctuations. The cyclostationary detector instead tests for correlation between spectral components at a specific cycle-frequency offset — a property that stationary noise essentially never has, so even a very weak signal's tiny nonzero cyclic correlation can still be statistically distinguishable from the near-zero cyclic correlation of noise alone.

screens.prompt: Why does noise being 'stationary' (rather than cyclostationary) matter for detector performance?

choices.text: Stationary noise has essentially no spectral redundancy at any nonzero cycle frequency, so a cyclic detector sees very little to compete against there
Stationary noise has more power than cyclostationary signals, always
choices.feedback: Stationarity is about statistical structure, not about how much power the noise carries — a stationary process can have any power level.

choices.text: It means energy detectors and cyclic detectors always give identical results
choices.feedback: They generally give different results precisely because they test different properties (total power vs. cyclic correlation) — that difference is the whole reason cyclic detection helps at low SNR.

screens.successFeedback: Right — the near-total absence of cyclic correlation in stationary noise, at any α ≠ 0, is exactly what lets a cyclic detector pull a weak structured signal out where an energy detector cannot.
